source_metadata.title_raw: Geoff Straw - A-20-031 - March 6, 2020 - San Luis Obispo

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 6, 2020

Geoff Straw
Executive Director
San Luis Obispo Regional Transit Authority
179 Cross Street, Suite A
San Luis Obispo, CA 93401

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-20-031

Dear Mr. Straw:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code section 1090
("Section 1090").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under Section 1090, not under
other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest. Also, note
that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and
any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the
facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Luis Obispo County District
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written
response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against
any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prohibit the San Luis Obispo Regional Transit Authority ("RTA") from
contracting with Rincon to provide specified construction monitoring subconsultant services for
Kitchell CEM with respect to a bus maintenance facility project where Rincon provided California
Environmental Quality Act ("CEQA") technical environmental services for the same project?

CONCLUSION

No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the RTA from contracting with Rincon to provide
construction monitoring subconsultant services for Kitchell CEM with respect to the bus
maintenance facility project where Rincon already provided CEQA technical environmental
services for that project.

¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-20-031
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The RTA is a Joint Powers Authority ("JPA") in San Luis Obispo County that operates
public transit service connecting cities throughout San Luis Obispo County and beyond. On
February 3, 2016, RTA issued a Request for Proposals ("RFP") for environmental planning services
to prepare California Environmental Quality Act ("CEQA") and National Environmental Policy Act
("NEPA") documentation for the RTA Bus Maintenance Facility project. The project involved the
construction of an approximately 45,000 square-foot, two-story combined administration
headquarters and bus maintenance building.

The San Luis Obispo Council of Governments ("SLOCOG"), the Federal Transit
Administration's ("FTA") designated recipient for federal transit funding in San Luis Obispo
County, worked in collaboration with the RTA to provide oversight for the project. A review
committee comprised of RTA, SLOCOG, and City of San Luis Obispo staff considered proposals
from several environmental consulting firms.

On April 26, 2016, the RTA retained Rincon to provide the requested environmental
services. As described in the RTA-prepared RFP and the agreement, all work deliverables were
subject to the approval of the RTA and FTA. Rincon prepared a CEQA compliant Initial Study
Mitigated Negative Declaration ("IS-MND") over a period lasting from April 2016 to September
2017. The IS-MND identified project impacts and determined that all impacts could be reduced to a
less than significant level with the incorporation of industry-standard mitigation measures.

Rincon evaluated the environmental impacts including its design but did not provide design
services or advise RTA on the design of the project. The RTA Board of Directors adopted the IS-
MND and approved the project on September 6, 2017. A NEPA categorical exemption was
subsequently granted by the FTA based on the IS-MND findings. Project design changes occurred
and included a reduction in overall size to approximately 34,800 square feet, a reduction of the
building height to a single-story with an interior mezzanine, and deletion of a separate building for
bus washing.

The RTA retained Stantec Architecture, Inc. ("Stantec") to design the project changes.
Stantec retained Rincon as its subconsultant to prepare a CEQA-compliant Addendum to the IS-
MND for the Project. The Addendum did not identify any new or more severe impacts, or new
mitigation measures. The Addendum and the revised project were approved by the City of San Luis
Obispo on July 24, 2019. The RTA did not contract with Rincon for any other projects over the
time period that Rincon provided environmental services for the maintenance facility project.

On September 23, 2019, the RTA released an RFP for Construction Management Services.
The RFP specified that prior to construction contract award, the Construction Manager ("CM") is
responsible for verification that the contract milestones are compatible with the Integrated Master
Project Schedule ("IMPS"), special construction requirements, and constructability reviews. The
CM will also assist the RTA with finalization of front-end procurement documents and selection of
the construction contractor. During construction, the CM is responsible for monitoring and
documenting contract compliance with the drawings, specifications, and other requirements
including construction warranties, contract documentation and as-built record, affirmative action

File No. A-20-031
Page No. 3

and EEO, DBE participation, and contract close-out. Additionally, the CM is responsible for
overseeing contract administration procedures and construction safety and security.

The RTA selected Kitchell CEM to provide the CM services. Kitchell CEM requested that
Rincon provide biological, archaeological, and Native American related environmental services
prior to and during project construction as a subcontractor. Environmental services to be provided
include preparation of an Extended Phase I Archaeological Study, archaeological and Native
American monitoring for earthwork activities associated with the project, as well as a
preconstruction nesting bird survey and invasive plant species training.

The RTA is considering Rincon's retention to provide these services. In the IS-MND,
Rincon had determined that the project could impact archaeological resources. The IS-MND
concluded that standard mitigation measures set out in Section 106 National Historic Preservation
Act of 1966 and outlined in Caltrans Standard Environmental Reference protocols could be
employed. The RTA has determined that Rincon has substantial expertise in this area. Rincon's
services with regard to crafting the IS-MND have concluded.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the
making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48
Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38
Cal.3d 633, 646.)

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district,
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members."

Independent Contractors Subject to Section 1090

Courts have long found that independent contractors that serve in advisory positions that
have a potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency
are subject to Section 1090. (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186
Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; Schaefer v. Berinstein (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278, 291 ["statutes
prohibiting personal interests of public officers in public contracts are strictly enforced. [Citation.]
... "] A person merely in an advisory position to a city is affected by the conflicts of interest rule".].)
This long-standing rule was recently affirmed by the California Supreme Court (People v. Superior
Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230), and it applies equally to corporate consultants. (Davis v.
Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300.)

The purpose behind this inclusiveness of the definition is to ensure that independent
contractors who are essentially performing a public function, though temporarily, provide the same
"fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 74 (1965).)

File No. A-20-031
Page No. 4

In Hub City, the court stated that a person's status as an official under Section 1090 "turns on the
extent to which the person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a
capacity that demands the public trust." (Hub City, supra, 186 Cal.App.4th at p. 1125.)

Most recently in Sahlolbei, the California Supreme Court addressed the standards for
applying Section 1090's prohibitions to contracts made by a public entity's independent contractors,
consultants and advisors. (Sahlolbei, supra, 3 Cal.5th at p. 234.) Importantly, the Supreme Court
concluded that Section 1090's reference to "officers" applies to "outside advisors with
responsibilities for public contracting similar to those belonging to formal officers" (Id. at p. 237),
and held that not all independent contractors are covered by Section 1090 – liability extends only to
independent contractors who can be said to have been entrusted with "transact[ing] on behalf of the
Government" (Id. at p. 240 quoting Stigall, supra, 58 Cal.2d at p. 570.)

Under the facts provided, there is no suggestion that the RTA entrusted Rincon with
transacting on RTA's behalf. Indeed, the initial contract between the RTA and Rincon, which
described all work deliverables as subject to the approval of the RTA and FTA, did not require
Rincon to perform any public contracting duties that would bring it within the ambit of Section
1090. Instead, the agreement simply required Rincon to provide environmental services including
preparation of a CEQA compliance IS-MND, which concluded all impacts could be reduced to a less
than significant level with the incorporation of industry-standard mitigation measures. The RTA
Board of Directors ultimately adopted the IS-MND and approved the project. In addition, although
Rincon evaluated the environmental impacts of the project including its design, Rincon did not
provide design services or advise RTA on the design of the project.²

Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit Rincon from providing subconsultant services
to Kitchell CEM for the construction monitoring of IS-MND.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:aja

² Rincon's subsequent subconsultant work for Stantec to prepare a CEQA-compliant Addendum to the IS-
MND for the same project, and which did not identify any new impacts or mitigation measures, does not change the
result.

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit the San Luis Obispo Regional Transit Authority ("RT A") from 
contracting with Rincon to provide specified construction monitoring subconsultant services for 
Kitchell CEM with respect to a bus maintenance facility project where Rincon provided California 
Environmental Quality Act ("CEQA") technical environmental services for the same project?
sections.conclusion:
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the RTA from contracting with Rincon to provide 
construction monitoring subconsultant services for Kitchell CEM with respect to the bus 
maintenance facility project where Rincon already provided CEQA technical environmental 
services for that project. 
1

sections.analysis:
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their

Section 1090 provides, in part, that"[ m ]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district,

contracting with Rincon to provide specified construction monitoring subconsultant services for 
Kitchell CEM with respect to a bus maintenance facility project where Rincon provided California 
Environmental Quality Act ("CEQA") technical environmental services for the same project?

construction monitoring subconsultant services for Kitchell CEM with respect to the bus 
maintenance facility project where Rincon already provided CEQA technical environmental 
services for that project.
